title: The Old Bullock Dray
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! the shearing is all over,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the wool is coming down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I mean to get a wife, boys,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When I go up to town.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Everything that has two legs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Represents itself in view,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the little paddy-melon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the bucking kangaroo.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ CHORUS

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  So itן¿½s roll up your blankets,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  And letן¿½s make a push,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Iן¿½ll take you up the country,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  And show you the bush.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Iן¿½ll be bound you wonן¿½t get
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Such a chance another day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  So come and take possession
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ  Of my old bullock dray.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now, Iן¿½ve saved up a good cheque,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I mean to buy a team,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And when I get a wife, boys,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll be all-serene
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For calling at the depן¿½t.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They say thereן¿½s no delay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To get an off-sider
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the old bullock dray.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! weן¿½ll live like fighting cocks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For good living, Iן¿½m your man.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll have leather jacks, johnny cakes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And fritters in the pan;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or if youן¿½d like some fish
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll catch you some soon,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For weן¿½ll bob for barramundies
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Round the banks of a lagoon.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! yes, of beef and damper
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I take care we have enough,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And weן¿½ll boil in the bucket
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Such a whopper of a duff,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And our friends will dance
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the honour of the day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the music of the bells,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Around the old bullock dray.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! weן¿½ll have plenty girls,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We must mind that.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½ll be flash little Maggie,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And buckjumping Pat.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Thereן¿½ll be Stringy bark Joe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Green-hide Mike.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yes, my Colonials, just
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As many as you like.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now weן¿½ll stop all immigration,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We wonן¿½t need it any more;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Weן¿½ll be having young natives,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Twins by the score.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I wonder what the devil
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jack Robertson would say
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If he saw us promenading
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Round the old bullock dray.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! itן¿½s time I had an answer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ If thereן¿½s one to be had,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I wouldnן¿½t treat that steer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the body half as bad;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he takes as much notice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of me, upon my soul,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As that old blue stag
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Off-side in the pole.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! to tell a lot of lies,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know, it is a sin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But Iן¿½ll go up country
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And marry a black gin.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oh! ן¿½Baal gammon white feller,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This is what sheן¿½ll say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Budgery you
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And your old bullock dray.ן¿½
meaning:
This evocative piece by Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton) represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Banjo Paterson (Andrew Barton)'s bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
